notable.notable_blurb: Tulane Board president who announced school's integration

birth.notes: Wikipedia gives Aug 20, 1903; FindAGrave gives Aug 30, 1903 — minor discrepancy.

death.cause: Apparent heart attack at Ochsner Foundation Hospital upon learning of his wife Eugenie's death by asphyxiation in early-morning house fire that destroyed their Metairie home. Arson was suspected but never proven.

military_service.role: Too old to enlist; instead joined State Department civilian service 1942-1948. Wrote speeches for Dean Acheson, George Marshall, Harry S. Truman in support of Marshall Plan. Assistant Secretary of State for Public Affairs 1946.

narrative_blocks.header: Announced Tulane's racial integration weeks before death
narrative_blocks.body: In early 1963, as president of the Board of Administrators of Tulane Educational Fund, Joseph Merrick Jones announced that Tulane University would admit Black students beginning the spring semester — ending the school's segregation. Weeks later, on March 11, 1963, a fire swept his home in Metairie Louisiana, killing both him and his wife. Arson was suspected but never proven.

narrative_blocks.header: Marshall Plan speechwriter for Acheson, Marshall, Truman
narrative_blocks.body: Joining the U.S. State Department in 1942 (too old to enlist for combat), Jones rose to Assistant Secretary of State for Public Affairs in 1946. He wrote portions of major speeches for Secretary of State Dean Acheson, Secretary George C. Marshall, and President Harry S. Truman in support of the Marshall Plan for European reconstruction. He returned to private law practice in 1948.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Jones Walker — major Southern law firm namesake
narrative_blocks.body: As a senior partner of Jones, Flanders & Waechter (later reorganized in 1937), Jones founded the firm now known as Jones, Walker, Waechter, Poitevent, Carrère & Denègre L.L.P. — still one of the leading law firms in the American South. He was a Fellow of Yale University's Department of Political Science, an editor of Fortune magazine, and a special consultant to the United Nations.

narrative_blocks.header: Suspected-arson fire killed him and wife same day
narrative_blocks.body: A fire destroyed the Jones family home in suburban Metairie Louisiana in the pre-dawn hours of March 11, 1963 — his wife Eugenie died at the scene from asphyxiation. Learning of her death at Ochsner Foundation Hospital, Jones suffered an apparent heart attack and was pronounced dead the same day. Arson was suspected — coming weeks after his Tulane integration announcement — but was never proven.

narrative_blocks.header: Returned Polk-Jones line to Charleston Huger family
narrative_blocks.body: Eugenie Penick Jones married Killian Loew Huger Jr. on October 24, 1953 in Orleans Parish Louisiana — marrying back into the Charleston Huger family that her great-great-aunt Elizabeth Polk Huger had entered through marriage to William Elliott Huger 89 years earlier. The two Hugers (Killian's father Arthur Middleton Huger and William Elliott Huger) likely shared common Charleston ancestry though the precise relationship is not yet documented. The Polk-Huger family network reconverged through this 20th-century marriage.

parents.father_research_notes: Arthur Middleton Huger (1877-1924) — likely descended from Charleston Huger family; possible cousin of Col. John Middleton Huger (father of HD2600's husband Wm Elliott Huger). Not yet built as separate entry.
parents.mother_research_notes: Jeanne Nathalie Charbonnet Huger (1879-1945) — New Orleans Charbonnet family. Not yet built as separate entry.

notable.notable_blurb: Canal Barge Chairman; raced champion Chou Croute
notable.primary_url_label: National Rivers Hall of Fame inductee page

birth.notes: Per 2001 obit age 66 = b. ~1935. Wikipedia birth date not specified.

death.cause: lengthy battle with heart and kidney disease

narrative_blocks.header: Canal Barge Chairman; National Rivers Hall of Fame
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Merrick Jones Jr. joined his father's Canal Barge Company in 1957 and took the reins in 1963 after his parents' deaths in the Metairie house fire. He served as Chairman of the Board across forty years of active service in the marine transportation business, growing Canal Barge into one of the most successful river-cargo operators in the United States. He was inducted as an Achievement Award winner of the National Rivers Hall of Fame at the River Museum in Dubuque, Iowa.

Jones co-owned the 1972 champion sprinter Chou Croute with E.V. 'Tony' Benjamin III, Isidore Newman II, and Sam Israel Jr. She beat the speedy Icecapade in the Fall Highweight Handicap at Belmont Park — the same year Secretariat was Horse of the Year as a two-year-old. Across her career Chou Croute won 18 of 28 starts and was inducted into the Fair Grounds Racing Hall of Fame in 2000.

narrative_blocks.header: Took Canal Barge reins after parents' fire deaths
narrative_blocks.body: Both his parents died on March 11, 1963 in the suspected-arson Metairie house fire — his mother from asphyxiation, his father from heart attack on learning of her death. Joseph Jr. took over the family-founded Canal Barge Company that same year at age 28. He served the company for the remaining 38 years of his life, until his own death from heart and kidney disease on September 13, 2001.
